title: Machine learning-based design (of proteins, small molecules and beyond)
speaker: Jennifer Listgarten
abstract: Data-driven design is making headway into a number of application areas, including protein, small-molecule, and materials engineering. The design goal is to construct an object with desired properties, such as a protein that binds to a target more tightly than previously observed. To that end, costly experimental measurements are being replaced with calls to a high-capacity regression model trained on labeled data, which can be leveraged in an in silico search for promising design candidates.

proof_stages.label: Protein design as enormous discrete extrapolation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames protein and molecular design as searching a nearly infinite combinatorial sequence space, where nature's evolved structure and available measurements suggest that machine learning may guide useful extrapolation.

proof_stages.label: Directed evolution as the wet-lab baseline
proof_stages.summary: Directed evolution is reviewed as a parent-diversification-screen-selection loop that works like random greedy search and motivates replacing or augmenting parts of the loop computationally.

proof_stages.label: Black-box oracle design formulation
proof_stages.summary: The computational design task is posed as using a trained predictive oracle, ideally with calibrated uncertainty, to return a set of high-property sequences while respecting domain-specific constraints.

proof_stages.label: Model-based optimization over search distributions
proof_stages.summary: Instead of optimizing directly over sequences, the method optimizes a parameterized distribution over sequences, yielding an uncertainty-aware generative update related to estimation-of-distribution algorithms, rare-event methods, and EM.

proof_stages.label: Robustness to extrapolative oracle pathologies
proof_stages.summary: Because oracle uncertainty can become unreliable away from training data, the method adds a density-based probabilistic trust region so that search is modulated by how far proposed designs move from credible regions.

proof_stages.label: In silico evaluation under synthetic ground truth
proof_stages.summary: Since ordinary held-out validation cannot test designs in unseen regions, the speaker describes constructing simulated ground truths from real data and comparing optimization methods in these controlled settings.

proof_stages.label: Autofocused oracle adaptation
proof_stages.summary: The autofocus extension introduces an oracle gap between the true and proxy design objectives, alternately updates the search distribution and oracle parameters, and naturally yields a covariate-shift correction focused on the current search region.

proof_stages.label: Domain-specific deployment and open modeling choices
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion emphasizes that data generation, search-model family choice, and application targets such as gene delivery, GFP, and materials design must be instantiated differently across domains.

examples.name: Needle in a haystack performance
examples.note: The Paris needle-search story is used as an analogy for searching an enormous discrete molecular design space, not as a source of the method.
